🌊
Chapter 4 — Explanation TextWhy Bombs and Poison Destroy Our Reefs Forever

Why Bombs and Poison Destroy Our Reefs Forever

Understanding how destructive fishing damages coral reefs, fish populations, and island livelihoods through explanation text, passive voice, and cause-effect chains.

GenreExplanation Text
Local ContextSapuka · Karang Koko · Liukang Tangaya
Language FocusPassive Voice · Causal Chains
Final ProductExplanation Text + Infographic + Oral Explanation

Opening

01. Chapter Identity & Learning Mission

CHAPTER 4 — EXPLANATION TEXT

Why Bombs and Poison Destroy Our Reefs Forever

Understanding How Destructive Fishing Damages Coral Reefs, Fish Populations, and Island Livelihoods

Komponen Keterangan
Mata Pelajaran Bahasa Inggris
Kelas/Fase Kelas XI / Fase F
Genre Explanation Text
Tema Reef Destruction and Island Future
Judul Why Bombs and Poison Destroy Our Reefs Forever
Konteks Lokal SMA Negeri 6 Pangkajene dan Kepulauan, Pulau Sapuka, Liukang Tangaya, Pangkep
Fokus Isu Destructive fishing, coral reef destruction, poison fishing, fish habitat loss, fish population decline, fishermen’s livelihood
Pendekatan Genre-Based Approach/SFL, CLT, Deep Learning, Environmental Literacy, Scientific Literacy, Cause-Effect Reasoning
Produk Akhir Explanation paragraph/text + cause-effect diagram + oral scientific explanation + reef protection project
Skill Utama Listening, Reading & Viewing, Speaking, Writing, Grammar & Vocabulary in Use
Language Focus Passive voice, causal chains, technical vocabulary, temporal markers, hedging

Learning Mission

In this chapter, students learn how to use English to explain a serious environmental phenomenon in island life: how destructive fishing damages coral reefs and affects fish populations, fishermen, families, and the future of the island.

Students will listen to a short documentary narration, read explanation texts, analyze cause-effect chains, study passive voice and scientific vocabulary, explain processes orally, and write an explanation paragraph or short explanation text.

The focus is not to blame people. The focus is to understand the process clearly.

Protecting reefs means protecting fish, families, and the future of island life.

Safety and Ethics Note

This chapter discusses destructive fishing as an environmental problem. It does not teach how to do harmful or illegal fishing practices.

This chapter does not explain:

  • how explosives are made;
  • what materials are used to make explosives;
  • how poison or harmful substances are mixed;
  • how harmful substances are applied in fishing;
  • any operational steps that can be copied.

This chapter only explains:

  • how reef structures can be damaged;
  • how harmful fishing practices can affect marine organisms;
  • why fish lose shelter when reefs are damaged;
  • why coral reef recovery takes a long time;
  • how damage to reefs can affect fishermen and island families;
  • why students should understand and protect the sea.
BKoF

02. Situational Hook & Big Questions

Situational Hook

Read and Think

The explosion lasts less than a second. The damage to the reef takes over 100 years to recover — if it ever does.

Under the sea, a coral reef is not just a beautiful decoration. It is a living place. Small fish hide there. Young fish grow there. Many marine organisms depend on it for shelter, food, and protection.

When a harmful blast happens underwater, the sound and pressure may disappear quickly. However, the reef structure can be broken into pieces. Fish may scatter. Some marine organisms may die. The reef that once worked like a living city can become unstable rubble.

Poison fishing can also disturb the sea. Harmful substances may affect fish and other organisms around the reef. Even if the target fish are caught, the wider ecosystem may suffer. The problem does not stop with one fishing event. Over time, damaged reefs can lead to fewer shelters, fewer young fish, weaker fish populations, and harder days for fishermen.

For students in Sapuka, this issue is not far away. The sea is part of family life. Many families depend on fish, lobster, teripang, seaweed, kerapu, kakap, katamba, teri, baronang, and other sea resources. If reefs are damaged, the future of the sea becomes uncertain.

So the big question is:

What really happens under the sea when coral reefs are destroyed?

Tujuan Pembelajaran

Setelah mempelajari chapter ini, peserta didik mampu:

  1. memahami fungsi sosial explanation text;
  2. mengidentifikasi struktur explanation text: phenomenon, explanation sequence, dan closing;
  3. membedakan explanation text dari narrative, exposition, procedure, dan report text;
  4. mengidentifikasi cause-effect relationship dalam teks;
  5. memahami proses sederhana bagaimana bom ikan dan bius/racun ikan merusak ekosistem terumbu karang;
  6. menjelaskan hubungan antara terumbu karang, habitat ikan, populasi ikan, dan mata pencaharian nelayan;
  7. menggunakan passive voice untuk menjelaskan proses, misalnya the reef is destroyed by...;
  8. menggunakan causal chains seperti this causes..., which leads to..., dan resulting in...;
  9. menggunakan technical vocabulary seperti ecosystem, biodiversity, coral reef, detonation, poison, marine life, habitat, recovery, dan population;
  10. menggunakan temporal markers seperti initially, over time, eventually, after that, dan as the damage continues;
  11. menjelaskan proses/fenomena secara lisan menggunakan bahasa ilmiah sederhana;
  12. menulis explanation paragraph yang kohesif;
  13. membuat diagram sebab-akibat tentang kerusakan karang;
  14. merefleksikan nilai menjaga laut sebagai bagian dari menjaga masa depan keluarga.

Big Questions

  1. What happens to coral reefs when destructive fishing occurs?
  2. Why are coral reefs important for fish populations?
  3. How can a short explosion cause long-term damage?
  4. What happens to fish when reefs are destroyed?
  5. Why does reef recovery take a very long time?
  6. How does poison affect marine ecosystems?
  7. Why should students care about coral reefs?
  8. How is reef protection connected to fishermen’s future?
  9. How can explanation text help us understand environmental problems?
  10. How can Sapuka students explain this issue in English?
BKoF

03. Building Knowledge of the Field

PART A — BKoF: Building Knowledge of the Field

Picture Prediction

Bayangkan gambar berikut.

An underwater scene shows two sides of a coral reef. On the left, the reef is healthy. Small fish swim between coral branches. The water is clear. The reef looks like a busy underwater neighborhood. On the right, the reef is broken. There are pieces of dead coral on the sea floor. Fewer fish are visible. The water looks disturbed.

Diskusikan:

  1. What differences do you see between the healthy reef and the damaged reef?
  2. Why do small fish need coral reefs?
  3. What may happen if reef structures are broken?
  4. Why can reef damage affect fishermen?
  5. What words can describe the process of reef destruction?

Reef Ecosystem Brainstorming

Coral Reef Supports...

Area Examples
Marine life fish, young fish, small organisms, coral animals
Fishermen fish catch, family income, future livelihood
Island life food, identity, local economy, sea knowledge
Environment biodiversity, habitat, ecosystem balance

Discussion

  1. Why is a reef more than just “rocks under the sea”?
  2. What happens when fish lose shelter?
  3. How can reef damage affect people who live far from the reef?

Cause–Effect Sorting

Kelompokkan pernyataan berikut sebagai cause atau effect.

Sentence Cause / Effect
A reef structure is broken.
Fish lose shelter.
Destructive fishing occurs near a reef.
Young fish have fewer safe places.
Fish populations may decline over time.
Fishermen may catch fewer fish.
Coral recovery takes a long time.
Marine biodiversity is reduced.

Before–After Reef Observation

Before Damage

  • coral branches are stable;
  • small fish hide between corals;
  • young fish have shelter;
  • different organisms live together;
  • fishermen may find fish around the reef.

After Damage

  • coral structures are broken;
  • rubble may move on the sea floor;
  • small fish lose shelter;
  • fewer organisms stay in the area;
  • fish populations may decline over time.

Questions

  1. Which changes happen immediately?
  2. Which changes happen over time?
  3. Which effects are environmental?
  4. Which effects are social or economic?
  5. Why is explanation text useful for this topic?

Local Sea Life Discussion

In Sapuka and nearby island areas, the sea is connected to daily life. Families may depend on fish, lobster, teripang, seaweed, kerapu, kakap, katamba, teri, baronang, and other sea resources. Some local places such as Karang Koko and Pulau Karangan can help students imagine how coral and island environments are connected.

Discuss:

  1. What sea resources are important for families in your area?
  2. Why do young fish need safe habitats?
  3. How can reef damage affect tomorrow’s fish catch?
  4. Why does “protecting the reef” also mean “protecting the family”?

Mini Survey: What Do We Know About Coral Reefs?

Question Yes No Not Sure
Coral reefs are living ecosystems.
Many young fish use reefs as shelter.
Destructive fishing can damage reef structures.
Poison fishing can affect organisms beyond target fish.
Reef damage can affect fishermen’s future catch.
Coral reef recovery can take a very long time.

Misconception Check

Statement True / False / Needs Explanation Reason
Coral reefs are only stones under the sea.
Fish can always find another home immediately.
A short harmful action can create long-term damage.
If fish are caught quickly today, the sea is always healthy.
Poison fishing may affect more than one species.
Protecting reefs can protect fishermen’s future.

Vocabulary Preview

English Bahasa Indonesia Example
coral reef terumbu karang A coral reef is an important marine habitat.
ecosystem ekosistem A reef ecosystem supports many organisms.
biodiversity keanekaragaman hayati Coral reefs support marine biodiversity.
habitat habitat/tempat hidup Fish need safe habitats to grow.
destructive fishing penangkapan ikan yang merusak Destructive fishing can damage reefs.
harmful substances zat berbahaya Harmful substances can affect marine organisms.
recovery pemulihan Reef recovery may take a very long time.
population populasi Fish populations may decline over time.
livelihood mata pencaharian Many fishermen depend on the sea for their livelihood.
cause-effect chain rantai sebab-akibat Explanation text often uses cause-effect chains.
Hear

04. Listening: Documentary Narration

PART B — HEAR: Listening / Documentary Narration

Listening Activity

Title

Under the Sea: What Happens When Reefs Are Destroyed?

Pre-Listening Questions

Diskusikan sebelum mendengarkan.

  1. What do you think happens when a reef is broken?
  2. Why do small fish need coral reefs?
  3. How can damage under the sea affect families on land?
  4. What words do you expect to hear in a documentary about reefs?

Documentary Narration Script

Teacher/Audio Script:

Under the sea, a coral reef is a living system. It is not just a hard object on the sea floor. It is a home, a shelter, and a feeding area for many marine organisms. Small fish hide between coral branches. Young fish grow near the reef. Many species depend on the reef ecosystem.

When destructive fishing happens near a reef, the damage can begin immediately. If an underwater blast occurs, the pressure can break coral structures. The reef may be turned into pieces of rubble. Some organisms may die, and many fish may leave the area. The event may last for only a moment, but the damage can remain for many years.

Poison fishing can also disturb the ecosystem. Harmful substances may affect fish and other marine organisms around the reef. The problem is not only the fish that are caught. Other organisms may also be affected. As a result, the balance of the ecosystem can be disturbed.

After a reef is damaged, fish lose shelter. Young fish have fewer safe places to hide from predators. Over time, fewer young fish may survive. This can lead to lower fish populations. If fish populations decline, fishermen may need to travel farther or work harder to catch fish.

For island communities, reef damage is not only an environmental problem. It is also a family problem, a food problem, and a future problem. In places like Sapuka and Liukang Tangaya, many families are connected to the sea. Protecting reefs means protecting marine life, fishermen’s work, and the next generation.

The sea gives life to the island. When reefs are protected, fish have homes. When fish have homes, fishermen have hope. When fishermen have hope, families have a future.

While-Listening Task A — Main Ideas

Centang ide yang kamu dengar.

While-Listening Task B — Cause and Effect

Cause Effect
Coral structures are broken.
Fish lose shelter.
Young fish have fewer safe places.
Fish populations decline.
Fishermen catch fewer fish.

While-Listening Task C — Technical Vocabulary

Word Meaning
ecosystem
marine organisms
rubble
poison fishing
shelter
population
livelihood

While-Listening Task D — Temporal Markers

Listen and tick the temporal markers you hear.

Post-Listening Discussion

  1. What is the first thing that happens when a reef is damaged?
  2. Why do young fish need reef shelter?
  3. How can damage under the sea affect families on land?
  4. What sentence from the documentary is most powerful?
  5. How can students explain this issue to younger students?
Concept

05. Understanding Explanation Text

PART C — Understanding Explanation Text

Social Function of Explanation Text

Explanation text is used to explain how or why something happens. It shows a process, a phenomenon, or a cause-effect relationship.

The social function is to:

  1. explain how something happens;
  2. explain why something happens;
  3. show cause-effect relationships;
  4. describe a process or phenomenon;
  5. help readers understand scientific, social, or environmental issues.

In this chapter, explanation text helps students understand:

  • how coral reefs are damaged;
  • why fish need reef habitats;
  • how poison fishing affects marine ecosystems;
  • why fish populations decline when reefs die;
  • how reef destruction can affect fishermen’s future.

A strong explanation text does not only say:

“Destructive fishing is bad.”

It explains:

“When reef structures are broken, fish lose shelter. This causes fewer young fish to survive, which may lead to lower fish populations over time.”

Explanation Text vs Other Genres

Genre Purpose Structure Key Question Example Topic
Explanation Text Explains how/why something happens Phenomenon → Explanation Sequence → Closing How does it happen? Why does it happen? How reef damage reduces fish populations
Exposition Text Presents opinion and arguments Thesis → Arguments → Reiteration What is the writer’s opinion? Destructive fishing should be stopped
Procedure Text Gives steps to do something Goal → Things Needed → Steps → Tips How do we do it? How to clean the schoolyard
Report Text Presents factual information General Classification → Description What is it like in general? Coral reefs as marine ecosystems
Narrative Text Tells a story Orientation → Complication → Resolution What happened to the characters? A student who learns to protect the sea

Text Structure of Explanation Text

Structure Function Guiding Questions Example
Phenomenon Introduces the phenomenon being explained What phenomenon is explained? Coral reef destruction happens when harmful fishing practices damage reef structures and disturb marine life.
Explanation Sequence Explains the process or cause-effect chain What happens first? What happens next? What causes what? First, the reef structure is broken. Then, fish lose shelter. Over time, fish populations may decline.
Closing Concludes the explanation and shows importance Why does this matter? What should readers understand? For island communities, protecting reefs means protecting food, work, and the future.

Structure Details and Writing Tips

A. Phenomenon

Function:
Introduce the process or event that will be explained.

Guiding questions:

  • What phenomenon will I explain?
  • Where does it happen?
  • Why is it important?

Example:

Coral reef destruction happens when harmful fishing practices damage the reef structure and disturb marine life.

Common mistake:

Coral reefs are beautiful. I like coral reefs.

Better:

Coral reefs can be damaged when destructive fishing practices break their structure and disturb the ecosystem.

B. Explanation Sequence

Function:
Explain how the process happens step by step or cause by effect.

Guiding questions:

  • What happens first?
  • What is the immediate effect?
  • What happens over time?
  • What is the long-term impact?

Example:

Initially, the reef structure is damaged. Then, fish lose their shelter. Over time, fewer young fish survive, which may lead to lower fish populations.

C. Closing

Function:
Summarize the explanation and show why the phenomenon matters.

Example:

For island communities, protecting reefs means protecting marine life, fishermen’s livelihood, and the future of families.
See

06. Main Model Text

PART D — SEE: Main Model Text

Model Text 1 — Main Explanation Text

Why Bombs and Poison Destroy Our Reefs Forever

The explosion lasts less than a second. The damage to the reef takes over 100 years to recover — if it ever does. This sentence may sound dramatic, but it shows an important truth: a short harmful action under the sea can create a long environmental problem. Coral reefs grow slowly, support many forms of marine life, and provide shelter for young fish. When destructive fishing practices damage reefs, the effect does not stop with broken coral. It can move through the whole ecosystem and reach the lives of fishermen and island families.

Coral reef destruction happens when harmful fishing practices damage the reef structure and disturb marine life. In island areas such as Sapuka and Liukang Tangaya, reefs are important because many families are connected to the sea. Fish, lobster, teripang, kerapu, kakap, katamba, teri, baronang, and other sea resources are part of local life. Reefs such as Karang Koko can be imagined as underwater neighborhoods where marine organisms live, hide, feed, and reproduce. When these places are damaged, the sea loses more than beauty. It loses habitat.

Initially, destructive fishing can create immediate physical damage. When an underwater blast happens near a reef, the pressure can break coral structures. Branching corals can be snapped, hard surfaces can be cracked, and the reef can be turned into loose pieces. The blast itself may be very short, but the broken structure remains. This is important because a reef is not only a surface. It is a complex shape with holes, branches, and spaces. These spaces are used by fish and small organisms as shelter. When the structure is broken, the shelter is destroyed.

After the reef structure is broken, fish lose places to hide. Young fish are especially affected because they need safe spaces to avoid predators. A healthy reef works like a nursery for many marine species. It gives young fish a place to grow before they move to wider waters. When the reef becomes unstable rubble, many of these safe places disappear. This causes young fish to become more vulnerable. As a result, fewer young fish may survive.

Poison fishing can disturb the ecosystem in another way. Harmful substances used in fishing may affect fish and other marine organisms around the reef. The problem is not only the target fish. Other organisms in the area may also be affected because an ecosystem is connected. If one group of organisms is harmed, other parts of the food chain may also change. In many cases, harmful substances can weaken the balance of the reef ecosystem. This can lead to fewer healthy organisms living around the reef.

As the damage continues, fish populations may decline over time. This decline does not always happen in one day. At first, fish may simply move away from the damaged area. Then, young fish may have fewer places to survive. Over time, fewer young fish grow into adult fish. Eventually, fishermen may notice that they need to travel farther, spend more time, or work harder to catch fish. This is how reef damage becomes a social problem. What begins under the sea can affect food, work, and family income on land.

Reef recovery is difficult because coral grows slowly and damaged reef surfaces may not easily support new growth. If a reef is broken into unstable rubble, young corals may have difficulty attaching and growing. Waves and currents can move broken pieces again and again. This movement can disturb new coral growth. In the long term, the reef may remain damaged for many years. Some areas may recover slowly if they are protected, but some damage may last for generations.

For Sapuka students, this explanation matters because the sea is not far from their lives. The sea is connected to family work, food, transport, and identity. Pulau Karangan, Karang Koko, the fishing boats near the dermaga, the west coast affected by abrasion, and even Sumur Seribu as part of island survival all remind us that land, water, and sea are connected. When the sea is damaged, island life is affected.

In conclusion, destructive fishing can damage coral reefs through a chain of effects. First, reef structures are broken or disturbed. Then, fish lose shelter and young fish become more vulnerable. Over time, fish populations may decline, and fishermen may face harder futures. Poison fishing may also disturb marine organisms and weaken ecosystem balance. Therefore, protecting reefs is not only about saving coral. It is about protecting fish, fishermen, families, and the future of island communities.

Mini Glossary from the Main Text

Word/Phrase Arti Example
reef structure struktur karang Reef structure provides shelter for fish.
marine life kehidupan laut Marine life is affected when reefs are damaged.
ecosystem ekosistem A coral reef is a living ecosystem.
habitat tempat hidup Fish need reef habitat to survive.
vulnerable rentan Young fish become vulnerable without shelter.
food chain rantai makanan Poison can disturb the food chain.
decline menurun Fish populations may decline over time.
rubble pecahan/reruntuhan Broken reefs can become unstable rubble.
recover pulih Coral reefs may take a long time to recover.
livelihood mata pencaharian Fishermen’s livelihood depends on healthy seas.

Comprehension Questions

  1. What phenomenon is explained in the text?
  2. Why are coral reefs important for young fish?
  3. What happens immediately when reef structures are broken?
  4. How can poison fishing disturb marine ecosystems?
  5. Why do fish populations decline over time after reef damage?
  6. Why is reef recovery difficult?
  7. How does reef damage affect fishermen and families?
  8. What local places or contexts are mentioned in the text?
  9. Which sentence shows the strongest cause-effect chain?
  10. What is the closing message of the text?

Structure Analysis

Paragraph Structure Main Idea Evidence / Explanation
1 Phenomenon / Hook A short harmful action can create long-term reef damage.
2 Phenomenon Reef destruction affects habitat and local life.
3 Explanation Sequence 1 Underwater blasts can break reef structures.
4 Explanation Sequence 2 Fish lose shelter when reefs are broken.
5 Explanation Sequence 3 Poison fishing can disturb organisms and food chains.
6 Explanation Sequence 4 Fish populations may decline over time.
7 Explanation Sequence 5 Reef recovery is difficult and slow.
8 Local Connection Sea damage affects Sapuka island life.
9 Closing Protecting reefs protects fish, families, and the future.

Cause-Effect Chain Table

Stage Cause Effect
1 Destructive fishing happens near a reef.
2 Reef structures are broken.
3 Fish lose shelter.
4 Young fish become more vulnerable.
5 Fewer young fish survive.
6 Fish populations decline.
7 Fishermen catch fewer fish.
8 Family livelihood is affected.
Reading

07. Core Model Texts

PART E — Core Model Explanation Texts

Model Text 2

How Coral Reefs Become Fish Homes

Coral reefs become fish homes because their structures provide shelter, food, and safe spaces for many marine organisms. A reef is not a flat surface. It has branches, holes, small caves, and hard surfaces. These shapes create many spaces where fish can hide, rest, and grow.

First, the reef structure gives shelter to small fish. Young fish are often vulnerable because they can be eaten by bigger fish. When they live near coral branches or small holes, they can hide more easily. This shelter increases their chance of survival.

Next, coral reefs support food sources. Many small organisms live around reefs. These organisms become part of the food chain. When different species live together, the reef becomes a rich feeding area. As a result, many fish stay near reefs because food is available.

Then, reefs become breeding and nursery areas for some marine species. Young fish need calm and protected places to grow. If the reef is healthy, it can support many stages of fish life. Over time, young fish can grow into adult fish and move to wider waters.

When reefs are damaged, this system is disturbed. Shelter is reduced, food sources may change, and young fish may have fewer safe places. This can lead to lower fish survival and eventually affect fish populations.

In conclusion, coral reefs become fish homes because they provide shelter, food, and nursery areas. Protecting reefs means protecting the places where fish begin their lives.

Structure

Part Content
Phenomenon Coral reefs become fish homes.
Explanation Sequence Reef structure provides shelter, food, and nursery areas.
Closing Protecting reefs protects fish homes.

Cause-Effect Table

Cause Effect
Reefs have branches and holes. Small fish can hide.
Small organisms live around reefs. Fish can find food.
Reefs provide protected spaces. Young fish can grow.
Reefs are damaged. Fish lose shelter and food sources.

Model Text 3

What Happens to Fish Populations When Reefs Die?

When coral reefs die, fish populations can decline through a chain of effects. This process does not always happen immediately, but it can become serious over time. Coral reefs are important habitats, especially for young fish. When the habitat is damaged, fish survival becomes more difficult.

Initially, the reef loses its living structure. Healthy coral areas provide hiding places and feeding spaces. When corals die or break, these spaces become fewer. As a result, young fish have fewer places to hide from predators.

Then, fewer young fish survive. This is important because young fish are the future adult fish. If fewer young fish survive each year, the number of adult fish can decrease later. This can reduce the fish population around the reef area.

After that, the food chain may also change. Some organisms that depend on healthy reefs may disappear or move away. When food sources change, fish behavior can also change. Some fish may leave the damaged area to find better habitat.

Over time, fishermen may notice the impact. They may need to travel farther to find fish. They may spend more time and fuel. They may also catch fewer fish than before. This shows that reef death is not only an underwater problem. It is connected to family livelihood.

In conclusion, when reefs die, fish populations can decline because fish lose shelter, young fish survive less often, food chains change, and fishermen are affected. Protecting reefs helps protect fish populations and island families.

Cause-Effect Table

Cause Immediate Effect Long-Term Effect
Reefs die. Fish lose shelter. Fish populations decline.
Young fish have fewer hiding places. More young fish are eaten. Fewer adult fish later.
Food sources change. Fish move away. Fishermen catch fewer fish.

Model Text 4

How Poison Fishing Disturbs Marine Ecosystems

Poison fishing disturbs marine ecosystems because harmful substances can affect more organisms than the target fish. In a reef ecosystem, fish, coral, small animals, plants, and microorganisms are connected. When one part is disturbed, other parts may also be affected.

First, harmful substances enter the water around the fishing area. These substances may affect fish behavior and body function. Some fish may become weak or unable to move normally. However, the effect may not stop with the target fish.

Next, other marine organisms in the area may also be affected. Small organisms, reef animals, and young fish can be sensitive to changes in water quality. If these organisms are harmed, the food chain can be disturbed. This causes changes in the balance of the ecosystem.

Then, coral reefs may become weaker. Even when the reef structure is not immediately broken, the living organisms around the reef can be stressed. Over time, repeated disturbance can reduce the health of the reef ecosystem.

After that, fish habitats may become less productive. If small organisms decrease and reef health is reduced, fewer fish may stay in the area. This can lead to lower fish populations and eventually affect fishermen.

In conclusion, poison fishing disturbs marine ecosystems by affecting fish, other organisms, water quality, food chains, and reef health. The damage may not always be visible immediately, but it can create long-term problems for the sea.

Safety Note: This text explains environmental impact only. It does not explain materials, methods, or procedures for harmful fishing.

Model Text 5

Why Reef Recovery Takes a Very Long Time

Reef recovery takes a very long time because coral growth is slow, damaged surfaces can be unstable, and the ecosystem needs many conditions to become healthy again. A reef is not rebuilt in a few days or months. It is created by living organisms over long periods.

Initially, when a reef is damaged, the coral structure may be broken into pieces. These broken pieces can become unstable rubble. If waves and currents move the rubble again and again, young corals may not attach easily. Without a stable surface, coral growth becomes difficult.

Next, coral larvae need suitable places to settle. If the damaged area is covered by unstable pieces, sediment, or algae, new coral growth may be limited. This means that the reef may stay damaged even after the harmful action has stopped.

Then, the ecosystem needs time to bring back different organisms. Fish, small animals, and coral species do not return all at once. Some organisms may come back earlier, while others may need more stable conditions. This makes reef recovery a slow process.

Over time, protection can help. If the damaged area is not disturbed again, coral and marine life may slowly return. However, repeated damage can stop recovery. When a reef is damaged again and again, the ecosystem has little time to heal.

In conclusion, reef recovery takes a very long time because coral grows slowly, damaged surfaces may be unstable, and the whole ecosystem needs time and protection. This is why preventing reef destruction is much better than trying to repair it later.

Model Text 6

How Protecting Karang Koko Protects Island Life

Protecting Karang Koko protects island life because coral areas support fish, fishermen, families, and the future of the community. For students in Sapuka, a local reef is not only an underwater place. It is connected to food, work, and local identity.

First, a coral area provides habitat for marine life. Fish need places to hide, feed, and grow. When coral structures are healthy, young fish have more shelter. This increases their chance of survival.

Next, healthy reefs can support fishermen’s work. If fish populations around reef areas remain healthy, fishermen may have better chances to catch fish in the future. This does not mean that fishing is always easy, but it shows that habitat protection is connected to livelihood.

Then, reef protection helps the wider island ecosystem. The sea, the coast, Pulau Karangan, Sumur Seribu, the west coast of Sapuka, and the daily life of families are connected through water, weather, food, and human activity. When one part of the environment is damaged, other parts may also feel the effect.

After that, students can become part of reef protection through learning and communication. They can explain why coral reefs matter, create posters, write explanation texts, and remind others that harmful fishing practices damage the future of the island.

In conclusion, protecting Karang Koko means protecting more than coral. It means protecting fish habitats, fishermen’s livelihoods, family futures, and the identity of island communities.

Lab

08. Cause-Effect Relationship Lab

PART F — Cause-Effect Relationship Lab

Understanding Cause and Effect

Term Meaning Example
Cause Why something happens Reef structures are broken.
Effect What happens because of the cause Fish lose shelter.
Chain effect A series of connected effects Reef damage → habitat loss → fewer fish → fishermen affected
Immediate effect Effect that happens quickly Coral branches are broken.
Long-term effect Effect that happens over time Fish populations decline.

Example Chain

Simple Chain

A reef is damaged.
↓
Fish lose shelter.
↓
Young fish become vulnerable.
↓
Fewer young fish survive.
↓
Fish populations decline.
↓
Fishermen may catch fewer fish.

Scientific Sentence Version

When a reef is damaged, fish lose shelter. This causes young fish to become more vulnerable, which can lead to lower survival rates. Over time, fish populations may decline, resulting in fewer fish for fishermen.

Match Cause and Effect

Cause Effect
1. Coral structures are broken. a. Fishermen may catch fewer fish.
2. Fish lose shelter. b. Young fish become more vulnerable.
3. Young fish survive less often. c. Fish have fewer hiding places.
4. Fish populations decline. d. Fewer adult fish appear later.
5. Harmful substances enter the water. e. Marine organisms may be affected.

Complete the Cause-Effect Chain

Destructive fishing happens near a reef.
↓
The reef structure is ____________________.
↓
Fish lose ____________________.
↓
Young fish become ____________________.
↓
Fish populations may ____________________.
↓
Fishermen may catch ____________________.
↓
Family livelihood may be ____________________.

Immediate vs Long-Term Effects

Effect Immediate / Long-Term
Coral branches are broken.
Fish scatter from the area.
Young fish lose shelter.
Fish populations decline.
Fishermen travel farther.
Reef recovery takes many years.
Family income may be affected.

Rewrite into Scientific Explanation

  1. The reef breaks. Fish have no home.

→ _______________________________________________________

  1. Poison affects fish. The sea becomes unhealthy.

→ _______________________________________________________

  1. Young fish die. Adult fish become fewer.

→ _______________________________________________________

  1. The reef takes long to grow. We must protect it.

→ _______________________________________________________

  1. Fishermen catch less fish. Families have problems.

→ _______________________________________________________

Build a Chain from Reef Damage to Family Livelihood

Use the prompts.

  1. When the reef is damaged, _______________________________.
  2. This causes ____________________________________________.
  3. Over time, _____________________________________________.
  4. As a result, ___________________________________________.
  5. In the long term, ______________________________________.
Grammar

09. Grammar and Language Lab

PART G — Language Features Overview

Language Feature Function Example
Passive Voice Focuses on the process or affected object The reef is damaged by destructive fishing.
Causal Chains Shows connected causes and effects This causes fish to lose shelter, which leads to fewer young fish.
Technical Vocabulary Makes explanation more scientific ecosystem, biodiversity, habitat, population
Temporal Markers Shows time/order in process initially, over time, eventually
Simple Present Explains general facts/processes Coral reefs support marine life.
Hedging Makes claims careful Reef damage can reduce fish populations.

PART H — Grammar and Language Lab

Passive Voice for Processes

Function

Passive voice is useful in explanation text because it focuses on what happens to something. In this chapter, passive voice helps explain what happens to reefs, fish habitats, and marine life.

Pattern

Subject + is/are + past participle + by + agent/cause

Examples:

  • The reef is damaged by destructive fishing.
  • Fish habitats are destroyed by harmful practices.

Examples in Context

  1. The reef is damaged by destructive fishing.
  2. Coral structures are broken by underwater blasts.
  3. Fish habitats are destroyed by harmful practices.
  4. Marine life is affected by poison.
  5. Young fish are exposed to predators when shelter is lost.
  6. The ecosystem is disturbed by repeated damage.
  7. Food chains are changed by habitat loss.
  8. Reef recovery is slowed by unstable rubble.
  9. Coral growth is limited by repeated disturbance.
  10. Fishermen’s livelihood is affected by declining fish populations.
  11. Karang Koko can be protected by community awareness.
  12. The sea is harmed when reefs are destroyed.
  13. Local families are affected when fish become fewer.
  14. The reef area is weakened by harmful fishing practices.
  15. The future of the island is threatened when marine habitats are damaged.

Common Mistakes

Incorrect Correct Explanation
The reef damaged by destructive fishing. The reef is damaged by destructive fishing. Add is/are before past participle.
Fish habitats is destroyed. Fish habitats are destroyed. Plural subject uses are.
Marine life is affect by poison. Marine life is affected by poison. Use past participle.
Coral structures are break. Coral structures are broken. Use past participle.

Practice A — Choose the Correct Passive Form

  1. The reef ________ by destructive fishing.

a. is damaged b. are damaged c. damages

  1. Fish habitats ________ by harmful practices.

a. is destroyed b. are destroyed c. destroy

  1. Marine life ________ by poison.

a. is affected b. are affect c. affects

  1. Coral structures ________ by underwater pressure.

a. are broken b. is break c. breaks

  1. Fishermen’s livelihood ________ by declining fish populations.

a. is affected b. are affected c. affect

Causal Chains

Function

Causal chains connect one event to another. They help readers understand how one cause creates several effects.

Connector Arti Example
this causes ini menyebabkan This causes fish to lose shelter.
which leads to yang menyebabkan Fish lose shelter, which leads to lower survival.
resulting in mengakibatkan Young fish survive less, resulting in fewer adult fish.
as a result akibatnya As a result, fish populations decline.
therefore oleh karena itu Therefore, reef protection is important.
consequently akibatnya Consequently, fishermen may catch fewer fish.

Examples

  1. The reef is broken, which causes fish to lose shelter.
  2. Fish lose their habitat, which leads to lower fish populations.
  3. Coral damage spreads over time, resulting in long-term ecosystem loss.
  4. Poison affects marine organisms; as a result, the food chain is disturbed.
  5. Young fish become vulnerable; therefore, fewer may survive.
  6. Fish populations decline; consequently, fishermen may catch fewer fish.
  7. Reefs become unstable, which leads to slower recovery.
  8. Habitat loss reduces fish survival, resulting in fewer adult fish.
  9. The reef is repeatedly damaged; as a result, recovery becomes more difficult.
  10. Protecting reefs keeps fish habitats safe, which supports fishermen’s future.

Temporal Markers

Temporal Marker Arti Example
initially pada awalnya Initially, the reef structure is damaged.
first pertama First, the coral branches are broken.
then kemudian Then, fish lose shelter.
after that setelah itu After that, young fish become more vulnerable.
over time seiring waktu Over time, fish populations decline.
eventually akhirnya Eventually, fishermen may catch fewer fish.
in the long term dalam jangka panjang In the long term, family livelihood can be affected.

Hedging for Scientific Carefulness

Hedging makes scientific explanations more careful. It helps writers avoid claims that are too strong.

Word/Phrase Arti Example
can dapat Destructive fishing can damage reefs.
may mungkin/dapat Poison may affect more than one species.
in many cases dalam banyak kasus In many cases, reef damage affects fish habitats.
over time seiring waktu Over time, reef damage can reduce fish populations.
it is possible that mungkin bahwa It is possible that fish move away from damaged areas.

Practice

Rewrite the sentences using hedging.

  1. Destructive fishing always destroys all fish.

Better: ___________________________________________________

  1. Poison kills every organism in the sea.

Better: ___________________________________________________

  1. Reefs never recover.

Better: ___________________________________________________

  1. Fishermen always lose all income when reefs are damaged.

Better: ___________________________________________________

  1. All damaged reefs are gone forever.

Better: ___________________________________________________

Vocabulary

10. Vocabulary in Use

PART I — Vocabulary in Use

A. Reef and Sea Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
coral reef terumbu karang Coral reefs support marine life.
reef structure struktur karang Reef structure gives fish shelter.
marine life kehidupan laut Marine life depends on healthy habitats.
fish habitat habitat ikan Fish habitat can be damaged by destructive fishing.
ecosystem ekosistem A reef is a complex ecosystem.
biodiversity keanekaragaman hayati Reefs support biodiversity.
sea floor dasar laut Broken coral may fall on the sea floor.
coral branch cabang karang Coral branches can shelter small fish.
reef recovery pemulihan karang Reef recovery may take many years.
marine organism organisme laut Marine organisms are connected in the ecosystem.
young fish ikan muda Young fish need safe shelter.
shelter tempat berlindung Reefs provide shelter.
rubble pecahan/reruntuhan Broken reefs can become rubble.
predator pemangsa Young fish hide from predators.
nursery area area pembesaran Reefs can be nursery areas for fish.

B. Destructive Fishing Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
destructive fishing penangkapan ikan yang merusak Destructive fishing can damage reefs.
explosive fishing penangkapan ikan dengan bahan peledak Explosive fishing can break reef structures.
poison fishing penangkapan ikan dengan racun/bius Poison fishing can disturb marine ecosystems.
harmful practice praktik berbahaya Harmful practices should be stopped.
illegal fishing penangkapan ikan ilegal Illegal fishing threatens marine resources.
underwater blast ledakan bawah air An underwater blast can break coral.
harmful substances zat berbahaya Harmful substances can affect marine organisms.
reef damage kerusakan karang Reef damage affects fish habitat.
habitat destruction perusakan habitat Habitat destruction can reduce fish populations.
ecosystem disturbance gangguan ekosistem Ecosystem disturbance can spread over time.

C. Cause-Effect Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
cause sebab/menyebabkan Reef damage causes habitat loss.
effect akibat Habitat loss is an effect of reef damage.
lead to menyebabkan Habitat loss can lead to fewer fish.
result in mengakibatkan Lower survival can result in fewer adult fish.
damage merusak Harmful practices damage reefs.
destroy menghancurkan Destructive fishing can destroy habitats.
reduce mengurangi Reef damage can reduce fish populations.
disturb mengganggu Poison can disturb the ecosystem.
affect memengaruhi Fish populations affect fishermen.
recover pulih Reefs recover slowly.
decline menurun Fish populations may decline.
weaken melemahkan Repeated damage can weaken the ecosystem.
break mematahkan/merusak Pressure can break coral structures.
lose kehilangan Fish lose shelter.
support mendukung Reefs support marine life.

D. Fish and Livelihood Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
fish population populasi ikan Fish populations may decline.
young fish ikan muda Young fish need shelter.
adult fish ikan dewasa Young fish become adult fish over time.
shelter tempat berlindung Reefs provide shelter.
breeding area area berkembang biak Reefs can support breeding areas.
food chain rantai makanan Food chains can be disturbed.
fishermen nelayan Fishermen depend on healthy seas.
livelihood mata pencaharian Livelihood can be affected by reef damage.
family income pendapatan keluarga Family income may decline when fish become fewer.
future catch tangkapan masa depan Reef protection supports future catch.
fuel bahan bakar Fishermen may use more fuel if they travel farther.
sea resources sumber daya laut Sea resources support island families.
island family keluarga pulau Island families depend on the sea.
future generation generasi masa depan Future generations need healthy reefs.

E. Scientific Explanation Phrases

Phrase Arti Example
initially pada awalnya Initially, the reef is damaged.
over time seiring waktu Over time, fish populations decline.
eventually akhirnya Eventually, fishermen may catch fewer fish.
as a result akibatnya As a result, young fish lose shelter.
this process proses ini This process can reduce fish populations.
this condition kondisi ini This condition makes recovery difficult.
in the long term dalam jangka panjang In the long term, families may be affected.
this causes ini menyebabkan This causes habitat loss.
which leads to yang menyebabkan Habitat loss leads to fewer fish.
resulting in mengakibatkan This may result in lower fish populations.

F. Local Sapuka Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
Sapuka Pulau Sapuka Sapuka students live close to the sea.
Liukang Tangaya Kecamatan Liukang Tangaya Liukang Tangaya has many island communities.
Karang Koko referensi karang lokal Karang Koko can represent local reef life.
Pulau Karangan pulau kecil berisi karang Pulau Karangan reminds us of coral environments.
Sumur Seribu konteks air lokal Sumur Seribu shows the importance of water in island life.
island community masyarakat pulau Island communities depend on the sea.
fishing family keluarga nelayan Fishing families need healthy fish populations.
seaweed rumput laut Seaweed is part of local livelihood.
west coast pantai barat The west coast faces abrasion.
dermaga pier/harbor Fishermen leave from the dermaga.

Phrase Chunks for Speaking and Writing

Starting an Explanation

  • This text explains how __________ happens.
  • The phenomenon is __________.
  • __________ happens when __________.
  • This process begins when __________.

Explaining Cause and Effect

  • This causes __________.
  • This leads to __________.
  • As a result, __________.
  • Consequently, __________.
  • This can result in __________.

Explaining Time Sequence

  • Initially, __________.
  • Then, __________.
  • After that, __________.
  • Over time, __________.
  • Eventually, __________.

Using Passive Voice

  • The reef is damaged by __________.
  • Fish habitats are destroyed by __________.
  • Marine life is affected by __________.
  • The ecosystem is disturbed by __________.

Closing an Explanation

  • In conclusion, __________.
  • This shows that __________.
  • For island communities, __________.
  • Protecting reefs means __________.
Practice

11. Reading, Viewing, and Speaking Practice

PART J — Reading and Viewing Practice

Identify the Phenomenon

Title Phenomenon
Why Bombs and Poison Destroy Our Reefs Forever
How Coral Reefs Become Fish Homes
What Happens to Fish Populations When Reefs Die?
How Poison Fishing Disturbs Marine Ecosystems
Why Reef Recovery Takes a Very Long Time
How Protecting Karang Koko Protects Island Life

Identify Explanation Sequence

Read the chain and label each part.

Reef structures are broken.
↓
Fish lose shelter.
↓
Young fish become vulnerable.
↓
Fish populations decline.
↓
Fishermen are affected.

Part Explanation
First event
Immediate effect
Long-term effect
Social effect

Identify the Closing

Which sentence is the best closing for an explanation text?

  1. “I think all students must love coral reefs.”
  2. “For island communities, protecting reefs means protecting food, work, and the future.”
  3. “Once upon a time, there was a beautiful reef.”
  4. “First, prepare your tools and clean the area.”

Answer: _______

Reason: ______________________________________________________

Label the Before-After Reef Diagram

Use these labels:

  • healthy reef;
  • broken coral;
  • fish shelter;
  • unstable rubble;
  • young fish;
  • fewer fish;
  • damaged habitat;
  • recovery area.
Diagram Part Label
Colorful coral with fish
Broken coral pieces
Small fish hiding
Empty damaged area
Coral pieces on sea floor

Match Technical Vocabulary with Meaning

Vocabulary Meaning
ecosystem a. tempat hidup makhluk hidup
biodiversity b. pemulihan setelah kerusakan
habitat c. sistem kehidupan yang saling terhubung
recovery d. jumlah makhluk hidup dalam suatu kelompok
population e. keanekaragaman makhluk hidup

Identify Passive Voice

Underline the passive voice.

  1. The reef is damaged by destructive fishing.
  2. Fish habitats are destroyed by harmful practices.
  3. Marine life is affected by poison.
  4. The ecosystem is disturbed by repeated damage.
  5. Fishermen’s livelihood is affected by declining fish populations.

Identify Temporal Markers

Circle the temporal markers.

  1. Initially, the reef structure is damaged.
  2. Then, fish lose shelter.
  3. After that, young fish become more vulnerable.
  4. Over time, fish populations may decline.
  5. Eventually, fishermen may catch fewer fish.

PART K — Speaking: Explaining a Process

Pair Explanation

Topic

Why are coral reefs important for fishermen in Sapuka?

Speaking Frame

Coral reefs are important for fishermen because ____________________.
First, reefs provide ____________________.
Then, young fish ____________________.
Over time, healthy reefs ____________________.
As a result, fishermen ____________________.
Therefore, protecting reefs means ____________________.

Diagram Talk

Use a before-after reef diagram.

Instructions

  1. Point to the healthy reef.
  2. Explain what fish get from the reef.
  3. Point to the damaged reef.
  4. Explain what happens when the reef is damaged.
  5. Explain the long-term effect on fishermen.

Useful Sentences

  • This part shows a healthy reef.
  • Small fish use this area as shelter.
  • This part shows broken coral.
  • When the reef is damaged, fish lose their habitat.
  • Over time, fish populations may decline.

Cause-Effect Chain Talk

Explain the chain orally.

When reefs are damaged, fish lose shelter. This leads to...

Continue:

  1. When reefs are damaged, fish lose shelter.
  2. This leads to ____________________________________________.
  3. As a result, ____________________________________________.
  4. Over time, _____________________________________________.
  5. Eventually, ____________________________________________.

One-Minute Scientific Explanation

Good morning, everyone.
Today, I will explain ____________________.
The phenomenon happens when ____________________.
Initially, ____________________.
Then, ____________________.
After that, ____________________.
Over time, ____________________.
In conclusion, ____________________.
Thank you.

Speaking Rubric

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Clarity Explanation is very clear and easy to follow. Mostly clear. Sometimes unclear. Difficult to understand.
Sequence Process is explained in logical order. Mostly ordered. Some parts are out of order. No clear order.
Cause-Effect Language Uses cause-effect phrases effectively. Uses some phrases correctly. Limited use. Very limited.
Technical Vocabulary Uses relevant technical words correctly. Uses some technical words. Limited vocabulary. Almost no technical vocabulary.
Pronunciation Clear and confident. Mostly clear. Needs support. Hard to understand.
Visual Use Uses diagram effectively. Uses diagram somewhat. Limited visual support. Does not use visual support.
Writing

12. Writing Workshop

PART L — Writing Workshop

Step-by-Step Writing Process

Step 1 — Choose a Phenomenon

Choose one.

  • What happens to fish populations when reefs die?
  • How does destructive fishing damage coral reefs?
  • Why does reef recovery take a long time?
  • How does poison fishing disturb marine ecosystems?
  • Why are coral reefs important for fishermen?
  • How does protecting Karang Koko protect island life?

My phenomenon:

____________________________________________________________

Step 2 — Write the Phenomenon Statement

Use this pattern:

__________ happens when __________.

Example:

Coral reef destruction happens when harmful fishing practices damage reef structures and disturb marine life.

My sentence:

____________________________________________________________

Step 3 — List Causes

Cause Explanation

Step 4 — List Immediate Effects

Immediate Effect Why It Happens

Step 5 — List Long-Term Effects

Long-Term Effect Why It Matters

Step 6 — Build a Cause-Effect Chain

Phenomenon: ____________________
↓
Cause 1: ____________________
↓
Effect 1: ____________________
↓
Effect 2: ____________________
↓
Long-term effect: ____________________

Step 7 — Add Passive Voice

Write three passive voice sentences.

  1. The reef is ____________________________________________.
  2. Fish habitats are ______________________________________.
  3. Marine life is _________________________________________.

Step 8 — Add Temporal Markers

Use at least four:

  • initially;
  • then;
  • after that;
  • over time;
  • eventually;
  • in the long term.

My sentences:

  1. Initially, ______________________________________________.
  2. Then, __________________________________________________.
  3. Over time, _____________________________________________.
  4. Eventually, ____________________________________________.

Step 9 — Add Technical Vocabulary

Use at least five words:

  • coral reef;
  • ecosystem;
  • habitat;
  • marine life;
  • biodiversity;
  • population;
  • recovery;
  • food chain;
  • livelihood.

My technical words:

____________________________________________________________

Step 10 — Write a Closing Sentence

Use this pattern:

For island communities, protecting __________ means protecting __________.

My closing:

____________________________________________________________

Paragraph Template

Phenomenon:
__________ happens when ____________________________________.

Explanation sequence:
Initially, _________________________________________________.
This causes _______________________________________________.
Then, _____________________________________________________.
Over time, ________________________________________________.
As a result, ______________________________________________.

Closing:
In conclusion, ____________________________________________.

Editing Checklist

Before submitting your explanation text, check:

Writing

13. Joint Construction & Independent Writing

PART M — JCoT: Joint Construction of Text

Joint Construction Theme

What Happens to Fish Populations When Reefs Die?

Class Brainstorming Table

Question Class Ideas
What is the phenomenon?
What happens first?
What happens to fish shelter?
What happens to young fish?
What happens to fish populations over time?
How are fishermen affected?
What closing message should we write?

Joint Cause-Effect Chain

Reefs die.
↓
____________________________
↓
Young fish ____________________
↓
Fish populations ____________________
↓
Fishermen ____________________
↓
Families ____________________

Shared Paragraph Example

When reefs die, fish populations can decline through a chain of effects. Initially, the reef structure is damaged, and fish lose important shelter. This causes young fish to become more vulnerable to predators. Then, fewer young fish survive and grow into adult fish. Over time, the number of fish around the reef may decrease. As a result, fishermen may need to travel farther or work harder to catch fish. In conclusion, reef death does not only affect marine life; it can also affect fishermen and island families.

Revision Checklist for Joint Text

Question Yes / Not Yet Revision Needed
Is the phenomenon clear?
Is the sequence logical?
Are cause-effect links clear?
Is passive voice used?
Are temporal markers used?
Is the closing strong?

PART N — ICoT: Independent Writing Mission

Write an explanation paragraph or short explanation text about reef destruction and island life.

Choose One Topic

  1. What happens to fish populations when reefs die?
  2. How does destructive fishing damage coral reefs?
  3. Why does reef recovery take a long time?
  4. How does poison fishing disturb marine ecosystems?
  5. Why are coral reefs important for fishermen?
  6. How does protecting Karang Koko protect island life?

Length

  • Explanation paragraph: 180–250 words.
  • Short explanation text: 350–500 words.

Required Parts

Part Requirement
Phenomenon Clear introduction of the phenomenon
Explanation Sequence Logical cause-effect process
Closing Strong final message
Language Passive voice, causal chains, temporal markers
Vocabulary Technical words about reefs, fish, ecosystem, livelihood
Safety No harmful technical details
Local Connection Sapuka, Karang Koko, fishermen, island families, or sea life

Planning Sheet

Title

____________________________________________________________

Phenomenon

____________________________________________________________

Explanation Sequence

  1. Initially, ______________________________________________.
  2. Then, __________________________________________________.
  3. After that, ____________________________________________.
  4. Over time, _____________________________________________.
  5. Eventually, ____________________________________________.

Passive Voice Sentences

  1. __________________________________________________________
  2. __________________________________________________________
  3. __________________________________________________________

Technical Vocabulary I Will Use

  1. ____________________
  2. ____________________
  3. ____________________
  4. ____________________
  5. ____________________

Closing

____________________________________________________________

Self-Editing Checklist

Assess

14. Projects, Quiz, and Reflection

PART O — LRT: Linking Related Texts

Before-After Reef Diagram

Create a diagram with two sides.

Healthy Reef

Include labels:

  • fish shelter;
  • young fish;
  • coral branches;
  • marine biodiversity;
  • healthy habitat.

Damaged Reef

Include labels:

  • broken coral;
  • unstable rubble;
  • fewer fish;
  • damaged habitat;
  • slow recovery.

Cause-Effect Infographic

Title:

From Broken Reef to Fewer Fish

Required chain:

Destructive fishing
→ reef damage
→ habitat loss
→ fewer young fish survive
→ fish populations decline
→ fishermen are affected

Add one closing message:

Protecting reefs means protecting island families.

Documentary Narration Script

Opening:

Under the sea, a coral reef is ____________________.

Phenomenon:

Reef destruction happens when ____________________.

Explanation:

Initially, ____________________.
Then, ____________________.
Over time, ____________________.
Eventually, ____________________.

Closing:

Protecting reefs means ____________________.

PART P — Interactive Quiz Draft

Multiple Choice Questions

  1. What is the main purpose of explanation text?

a. To tell a story
b. To explain how or why something happens
c. To give steps to make something
d. To describe one person

  1. Which structure belongs to explanation text?

a. Thesis → arguments → reiteration
b. Goal → materials → steps
c. Phenomenon → explanation sequence → closing
d. Orientation → complication → resolution

  1. Which sentence shows cause and effect?

a. Coral reefs are beautiful.
b. Fish lose shelter, which leads to lower survival.
c. I like the sea.
d. The boat is blue.

  1. Which sentence uses passive voice?

a. Fish hide in reefs.
b. The reef is damaged by destructive fishing.
c. Fishermen go to sea.
d. Students study English.

  1. Which word is technical vocabulary?

a. happy
b. ecosystem
c. very
d. beautiful

  1. Which temporal marker shows a long-term process?

a. over time
b. yesterday
c. maybe
d. because

  1. What happens when fish lose shelter?

a. They become more protected.
b. Young fish may become more vulnerable.
c. Reefs grow faster.
d. Fishermen stop needing fish.

  1. Why should students use hedging?

a. To make claims careful
b. To make claims louder
c. To avoid grammar
d. To create a story

  1. Which phrase shows a causal chain?

a. once upon a time
b. this causes... which leads to... resulting in...
c. first, prepare tools
d. in my opinion

  1. What is the main message of this chapter?

a. Reefs are not connected to people.
b. Protecting reefs helps protect fish, fishermen, and island families.
c. Explanation text is only about rain.
d. Students cannot explain environmental problems.

True/False Questions

Write True or False.

  1. Explanation text explains how or why something happens.
  2. A phenomenon introduces the issue being explained.
  3. Explanation sequence shows a process or cause-effect chain.
  4. Closing is not needed in explanation text.
  5. Coral reefs can provide shelter for young fish.
  6. Reef damage can affect fishermen over time.
  7. Passive voice can be used to explain processes.
  8. This chapter teaches how to do destructive fishing.
  9. Temporal markers help show process order.
  10. Protecting reefs can protect island livelihoods.

Project / Digital Creation Task

Option A — Cause-Effect Infographic

Create an infographic titled:

How Reef Damage Affects Fish

Include:

  • one phenomenon;
  • one cause-effect chain;
  • at least five arrows;
  • three technical words;
  • one local message.

Option B — Before-After Reef Poster

Create a poster showing:

  • healthy reef;
  • damaged reef;
  • fish shelter;
  • habitat loss;
  • effect on fishermen.

Add one closing sentence:

Healthy reefs support island life.

Option C — Documentary Narration Script

Write a 1–2 minute documentary narration.

Title:

Under the Sea: Why Reefs Matter

Include:

  • opening hook;
  • phenomenon;
  • cause-effect sequence;
  • local connection;
  • closing message.

Option D — Oral Explanation Card

Create a small card to help you speak.

Include:

  • phenomenon;
  • 4 cause-effect sentences;
  • 3 technical words;
  • 1 closing message.

Option E — Reef Protection Campaign Poster

Create a poster with one slogan:

  • Protect Reefs, Protect Families
  • Healthy Reefs, Healthy Fish
  • Karang Koko Matters
  • Broken Reefs Mean Fewer Fish
  • Save the Reef, Save the Future

Include:

  • one explanation chain;
  • one local place/reference;
  • one student action.

PART R — Reflection

Final Reflection

Jawab dengan jujur dan reflektif.

Bahasa Indonesia

  1. Mengapa terumbu karang penting bagi kehidupan nelayan?
  2. Apa yang terjadi pada ikan ketika karang rusak?
  3. Mengapa kerusakan singkat bisa berdampak sangat lama?
  4. Apa hubungan menjaga laut dengan menjaga masa depan keluarga?
  5. Bagaimana saya bisa menjelaskan masalah ini dalam Bahasa Inggris?
  6. Apa satu pesan yang ingin saya sampaikan kepada masyarakat tentang laut?
  7. Mengapa kita perlu memahami proses, bukan hanya mengatakan “itu buruk”?
  8. Apa yang bisa dilakukan siswa untuk ikut menjaga laut secara aman dan positif?

English Prompts

Complete the sentences.

  1. Coral reefs are important because ____________________.
  2. When reefs are destroyed, ____________________.
  3. This causes ____________________.
  4. Over time, ____________________.
  5. Fishermen may be affected because ____________________.
  6. Protecting the sea means ____________________.
  7. One message I want to share is ____________________.
Teacher

15. Assessment Rubrics & Teacher Notes

PART S — Assessment Rubrics

Rubric for Explanation Writing

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Clear Phenomenon Phenomenon is clear, specific, and relevant. Phenomenon is clear. Phenomenon is general or unclear. Phenomenon is missing.
Explanation Sequence Sequence is logical and complete. Sequence is mostly logical. Some parts are confusing. No clear sequence.
Cause-Effect Clarity Cause-effect chain is strong and well connected. Cause-effect is clear. Cause-effect is limited. Cause-effect is unclear.
Passive Voice Passive voice is used accurately and meaningfully. Some passive voice is used correctly. Limited or inaccurate use. No passive voice.
Technical Vocabulary Uses relevant technical words correctly. Uses some technical words. Vocabulary is limited. Very limited vocabulary.
Temporal Markers Uses markers effectively. Uses some markers. Limited markers. No markers.
Cohesion Sentences connect smoothly. Mostly connected. Some jumps in ideas. Ideas are disconnected.
Closing Closing is meaningful and connected to the issue. Closing is clear. Closing is weak. Closing is missing.
Safety Avoids unsafe technical details completely. Safe and appropriate. Mostly safe but needs caution. Includes unsafe/inappropriate detail.
Local Relevance Strong connection to Sapuka/island life. Clear local connection. Limited local connection. No local connection.

Rubric for Cause-Effect Diagram

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Chain Accuracy Cause-effect chain is accurate and complete. Mostly accurate. Some links unclear. Chain is inaccurate.
Visual Organization Very clear and easy to follow. Clear. Somewhat messy. Difficult to follow.
Technical Vocabulary Uses technical labels correctly. Some technical labels. Limited vocabulary. No technical vocabulary.
Local Connection Strong local relevance. Some local relevance. Limited local relevance. No local relevance.
Message Strong environmental message. Clear message. Weak message. No message.

Rubric for Oral Explanation

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Clarity Explanation is very clear and confident. Mostly clear. Sometimes unclear. Difficult to understand.
Logical Sequence Process is well ordered. Mostly ordered. Some parts out of order. No clear order.
Cause-Effect Language Uses cause-effect language effectively. Uses some cause-effect phrases. Limited use. Very limited.
Technical Vocabulary Uses relevant vocabulary correctly. Uses some vocabulary. Limited vocabulary. Almost none.
Pronunciation Clear and understandable. Mostly understandable. Needs support. Hard to understand.
Visual Support Uses diagram/visual effectively. Uses visual somewhat. Limited use. No visual support.

Rubric for Project / Infographic / Poster

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Scientific Accuracy Information is accurate and safe. Mostly accurate. Some unclear parts. Inaccurate or unsafe.
Cause-Effect Chain Chain is clear and complete. Mostly clear. Limited chain. No clear chain.
Visual Organization Neat, readable, and attractive. Readable. Somewhat messy. Hard to read.
Language Accuracy English is accurate and appropriate. Some errors but clear. Many errors but understandable. Difficult to understand.
Local Relevance Strong Sapuka/island connection. Clear local connection. Limited connection. No connection.
Environmental Message Strong and meaningful. Clear. Weak. Missing.

PART T — Teacher Notes

Teaching Guidance

A. Discuss Destructive Fishing Safely

Do not explain materials, methods, or operational steps of harmful fishing. Keep the focus on:

  • reef damage;
  • fish habitat loss;
  • ecosystem disturbance;
  • long-term livelihood impact;
  • prevention and environmental responsibility.

B. Avoid Blaming Families or Fishermen Personally

Some students may come from fishing families. Avoid statements that shame families or communities. Use neutral language:

  • “harmful practices damage reefs”;
  • “communities need healthy reefs”;
  • “protecting reefs protects fishermen’s future.”

C. Use Local Context Carefully

Use Sapuka, Karang Koko, Pulau Karangan, Sumur Seribu, and west coast abrasion as context for environmental connection. Do not make unsupported data claims.

D. Teach Cause-Effect Visually

Use arrows, diagrams, and before-after visuals.

Example:

reef damage → habitat loss → lower young fish survival → fish population decline → fishermen affected

E. Teach Passive Voice Through Process

Do not teach passive voice only as a formula. Show why it is useful:

  • The reef is damaged.
  • Marine life is affected.
  • Fish habitats are destroyed.

The focus is on the affected object and process.

F. Support Students with Scientific Vocabulary

Use bilingual vocabulary tables, matching tasks, sentence frames, and diagram labels.

G. Remedial Ideas

For students who struggle:

  1. give them a cause-effect chain with missing words;
  2. let them explain orally before writing;
  3. allow them to write one paragraph only;
  4. provide passive voice sentence starters;
  5. use picture-based explanation.

H. Enrichment Ideas

For advanced students:

  1. write a longer explanation text;
  2. create a documentary narration;
  3. design a reef protection infographic;
  4. lead a one-minute scientific explanation;
  5. compare reef damage with another environmental phenomenon.

Closing Message for Students

Explanation text helps us understand what happens beneath the surface. A damaged reef is not only a broken underwater object. It is the beginning of a chain reaction.

When reefs are damaged, fish lose shelter. When fish lose shelter, young fish become vulnerable. When fewer young fish survive, fish populations may decline. When fish become fewer, fishermen and families may face a harder future.

This is why protecting the sea is not only an environmental message. It is a family message. It is a future message. It is a message for every island student who understands that the sea is part of home.

Protect the reef. Protect the fish. Protect the family. Protect the future.
